Name: adaptive_compute_allocation_multi_task
Title: Adaptive Compute Allocation in Multi-Task Learning via Activation Geometry
Short Hypothesis: Activation geometry (e.g., spectral decay and covariance structure) can serve as a robust proxy for task-specific computational difficulty, enabling dynamic compute allocation across tasks to enforce strict latency budgets without sacrificing accuracy.
Related Work: Existing work focuses on single-task scenarios or generic latency optimization frameworks. For example, 'Spectral Quantization Routing' (founder_1) uses activation geometry for precision allocation but does not address multi-task dynamics. Similarly, 'Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation' (founder_1) isolates task signals but does not explore adaptive compute allocation across tasks. This proposal uniquely bridges activation geometry with multi-task learning, offering a novel pathway for latency-aware inference.
Abstract: Multi-task learning (MTL) presents unique challenges for latency-critical inference, as tasks often vary significantly in computational difficulty. We propose a training-free framework that leverages activation geometry—specifically, spectral decay and covariance structure—to dynamically allocate compute resources across tasks. By computing geometric diagnostics on-the-fly (e.g., condition number, spectral decay rate), we derive a compute-throttling policy that routes tokens through fewer layers or prunes attention heads for computationally simpler tasks, enforcing strict wall-clock latency targets. We ground this mechanism in PAC-Bayes theory, linking geometric stability to generalization under compute perturbations. Evaluated on multi-task benchmarks in single-GPU setups, our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static baselines. This work bridges mechanistic interpretability with system-scale efficiency, offering a mathematically transparent method for resource-efficient MTL inference.
Experiments: Evaluate on multi-task benchmarks (e.g., GLUE, SuperGLUE) with controlled task difficulty variation.
Measure throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers under strict latency budgets.
Compare against static compute allocation baselines (e.g., fixed layer count, uniform pruning).
Quantify geometric diagnostics (e.g., condition number, spectral decay rate) and their correlation with task difficulty.
Validate PAC-Bayes generalization bounds under compute perturbations.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Computing geometric diagnostics on-the-fly may introduce overhead, though we aim to minimize this via randomized SVD.
The framework assumes tasks are separable based on activation geometry, which may not hold for highly entangled tasks.
PAC-Bayes bounds may be overly conservative, limiting practical utility.
